text: The UN innovation ecosystem can be mapped by function: entity-level implementation, system-level knowledge exchange, coordination channels, flexible delivery enablers, and adoption owners.
No single model dominates innovation delivery in the UN, supporting TAP as an orchestrator and execution-enabler.
Support for innovation breaks at ownership, technical delivery, funding, procurement/legal, resource sharing, and adoption.
The critical gap in the UN innovation ecosystem is system-level implementation capacity to mobilize entity capacity for system-wide use cases.
Grassroots ideas fail to achieve multi-entity adoption due to missing mandate, common owner, pooled funding, technical team, or sustainable service-provider route.
sourceIds: Talea interview (Global Pulse cohorts, Data Commons)

text: TAP can learn from at least five UN models: WFP Innovation Accelerator, UNICEF Venture Fund, Global Pulse accelerator, Digital ID/Data Commons, and CRAF'd/MPTF.
TAP should adapt substantive practices like stage gates, fast sprints, adoption-owner checks, structured technical support, portfolio-level measurement, and clear transition routes.
A TAP-worthy use case must be new or adapted, technology-enabled, have system-wide potential, and a real adoption pathway, excluding routine maintenance and one-entity tasks.
Main blockers for innovation include fragmentation, funding constraints, policy/legal/governance issues, limited technical capacity, weak adoption ownership, and culture/leadership gaps.
sourceIds: TAP survey slide in working draft

text: TAP support should change by maturity: discovery/demand, seed/pilot, implementation, scale, and transition stages.

TAP should triangulate demand signals across surveys, pipelines, job postings, pooled-funding signals, community inputs, and use-case progress data, prioritizing by system value and adoption readiness.
TAP's client is the accountable problem/adoption owner, not just the innovator; the client must commit to adoption, funding, legal/policy path, and transition.
TAP should use a hybrid model: a lean Coordination Unit, time-bound Surge Task Forces, the UN Innovation Ecosystem as source, a Steering Committee, and pooled funding.
TAP should adopt a catalytic, stage-based public-value venture posture with KPIs tracking portfolio flow, adoption, reuse, and integrity risks, not idea volume.
TAP should move from temporary UN80 governance into a standing joint programme or MPTF-style mechanism with key risks including coordination-only failure and duplication.
